summary: The authors leveraged an autonomous Kaggle agent called Qgentic-AI to rapidly design and execute a translation pipeline under severe time constraints. Instead of cleaning the provided competition training data, they generated high-quality transliteration/translation pairs using Gemini OCR on PDFs and by splitting published texts. They post-trained Qwen2.5-32B and Qwen2.5-72B models with LoRA on this synthetic data, alongside a ByT5-Base model, achieving a competitive rank through careful preprocessing, post-processing deduplication, and iterative agent-driven experimentation.
extensive_summary:
## Solution Design & Agent-Driven Workflow
The team operated under extreme time constraints (70-75 hour work weeks) and delegated high-level solution design to an autonomous Kaggle agent they developed, Qgentic-AI. The agent iteratively ran experiments, tuned hyperparameters, and distilled the workflow into structured Markdown files. This approach allowed them to compete effectively with only 6 days of active grinding.

## Data Generation & Strategy
A core strategic decision was explicitly rejecting the competition's provided training data, which the agent initially struggled to process. Instead, the authors generated approximately 55,000 high-quality transliteration/translation pairs through two main channels: splitting published texts into sentences and generating translations via Gemini, and feeding PDF pages directly into Gemini for OCR-based extraction. After manual cleaning and LLM-as-a-judge filtering, they created bidirectional training sets (~110,000 pairs total) for ENG -> AKK and AKK -> ENG translation.

## Model Training & Architecture
The primary models were Qwen2.5-32B and Qwen2.5-72B, post-trained for 3 epochs on 8x B200 GPUs using LoRA with rank 64 and alpha 128. A ByT5-Base model was also trained for 15 epochs at a learning rate of 3e-4. The agent handled parameter tuning, significantly reducing manual overhead.

## Preprocessing & Postprocessing
Preprocessing followed the host's discussion guidelines, fixing specific formatting bugs like converting "5/12 shekels" to "2/3 shekel 15 grains". The code heavily normalizes Roman numerals, decimals, ASCII fractions, and subscript numbers into Unicode equivalents, while standardizing gap markers (`<gap>`). Postprocessing involved removing repetitive phrases from model outputs using a custom prompt directed at the base Qwen2.5-72B model, acting as a text editor to clean translations without altering meaning.

## Validation & Limitations
Validation relied on a perturbed version of `train.csv` that removed samples with significant length mismatches between training and validation sets. The authors acknowledge data leakage in this CV setup (CV score ~43) and do not rely on it exclusively for model selection. Several approaches were explicitly abandoned, including larger ByT5 models, MBR, using the competition train data, Arabic intermediate translation, and training a separate judge model.
competition_flow: Raw PDFs and published texts were processed via Gemini OCR to generate synthetic transliteration/translation pairs, which were cleaned and used to post-train Qwen2.5 and ByT5 models with LoRA, followed by regex-based normalization and LLM-assisted deduplication before final submission.
data_reading: PDF pages and published texts were fed into Gemini for OCR and sentence splitting; competition CSV data was loaded via pandas for validation perturbation.
data_processing: External data generated via Gemini OCR on PDFs and published texts (~55k pairs).
Preprocessing fixed formatting bugs (e.g., fractions, Roman months, subscript numbers) and standardized gap markers using regex.
Postprocessing removed repetitions using a base Qwen2.5-72B prompt.
Validation set was perturbed by removing samples with large train/val length mismatches.
cv_strategy: Perturbed version of train.csv removing all bad samples where the training and validation lengths are way off.
ensembling: The solution relies on post-training two Qwen2.5 variants and a ByT5-Base model, with final outputs cleaned via regex normalization and LLM-assisted repetition removal rather than explicit model ensembling.
insights: Leveraging an autonomous agent for iterative experimentation drastically reduced development time under severe constraints.
Generating high-quality synthetic data via Gemini OCR and published texts outperformed attempting to clean the noisy competition training data.
Post-processing model outputs with a dedicated deduplication prompt significantly improved translation quality without retraining.
critical_findings: The competition's provided training data was deemed too confusing and was explicitly abandoned in favor of synthetic data.
Larger ByT5 models (>= Large) failed to improve results, contrary to typical scaling expectations.
Relying solely on the perturbed validation set was risky due to inherent data leakage, necessitating cautious model selection.
what_did_not_work: Training a judge model to grade the quality of the translations

other_solution_refs.description: Host's discussion thread referenced for preprocessing guidelines
